description: Additive amendment import for revised Welsh Foundations Mixed Confidence milestone lists. Contains only affected Foundations list rows and new duplicate word records.
notes: Importer upserts rows by id and does not delete omitted lists or words.
Existing mixed-confidence lists are disabled instead of shrunk in place, because omitted word rows would remain in the database.
New duplicate word records use existing live-export editorial metadata but leave audioUrl blank and audioStatus missing for the normal audio-generation workflow.
No primer screens are included on the new mixed-confidence lists.

lists.name: W
lists.description: Unlike English, Welsh often uses W as part of the vowel sound. This pattern appears throughout the language.

primerContent.bodyEn:
W often behaves like a vowel in Welsh.
It can sound a little like the “oo” in the English word “pool”.
When W comes before a vowel, it often sounds more like the W in the English word “wet”.
primerContent.bodyCy:
Mae W yn aml yn ymddwyn fel llafariad yn Gymraeg.
Gall swnio ychydig fel “oo” yn y gair Saesneg “pool”.
Pan ddaw W cyn llafariad, mae'n aml yn swnio'n fwy fel y W yn y gair Saesneg “wet”.

lists.name: CH
lists.description: CH is one of the most recognisable Welsh spelling patterns. It is not pronounced like the CH in the English word “church”.

primerContent.bodyEn:
CH is treated as a single Welsh sound.
It often sounds like the CH in the Scottish word “loch”.
primerContent.bodyCy:
Mae CH yn cael ei thrin fel un sain Gymraeg.
Mae'n aml yn swnio fel CH yn y gair Albanaidd “loch”.

lists.name: LL
lists.description: LL is one of the most distinctive Welsh spelling patterns. Many Welsh place names and everyday words contain LL. With practice, it quickly becomes easier to recognise.

primerContent.bodyEn:
LL is treated as its own Welsh sound.
It does not behave like two separate letters.
primerContent.bodyCy:
Mae LL yn cael ei thrin fel sain Gymraeg ei hun.
Nid yw'n ymddwyn fel dwy lythyren ar wahân.

lists.name: RH
lists.description: RH is a common Welsh spelling pattern. Once familiar, it becomes easy to spot in everyday Welsh.

primerContent.bodyEn:
RH is treated as its own Welsh sound.
It is often heard at the beginning of Welsh words.
primerContent.bodyCy:
Mae RH yn cael ei thrin fel sain Gymraeg ei hun.
Fe'i clywir yn aml ar ddechrau geiriau Cymraeg.

lists.name: AE / AI
lists.description: AI and AE appear in many common Welsh words. Although they are spelled differently, they often produce very similar sounds.

primerContent.bodyEn:
AI and AE often sound similar in Welsh.
They can sound a little like the English word “eye”.
primerContent.bodyCy:
Mae AI ac AE yn aml yn swnio'n debyg yn Gymraeg.
Gallant swnio ychydig fel y gair Saesneg “eye”.

lists.name: WY
lists.description: WY appears in a number of common Welsh words. The exact sound can vary in some situations, but these examples provide a useful starting point.

primerContent.bodyEn: WY often sounds similar to the “oi” in the English word “coin”.
primerContent.bodyCy: Mae WY yn aml yn swnio'n debyg i “oi” yn y gair Saesneg “coin”.

lists.name: YW
lists.description: YW is usually much more consistent than WY. Once familiar, it becomes easy to recognise in Welsh words.

primerContent.bodyEn: YW often sounds similar to the English word “you”.
primerContent.bodyCy: Mae YW yn aml yn swnio'n debyg i'r gair Saesneg “you”.

lists.name: OE
lists.description: OE is one of the more consistent Welsh vowel combinations. The same sound appears in many Welsh words.

primerContent.bodyEn: OE sounds similar to the “oy” in the English word “boy”.
primerContent.bodyCy: Mae OE yn swnio'n debyg i “oy” yn y gair Saesneg “boy”.

lists.name: AU
lists.description: AU appears in a variety of Welsh words. Although it may look unfamiliar at first, the sound quickly becomes recognisable.

primerContent.bodyEn: AU often sounds similar to the long English “I” sound in words such as “sky” or “tie”.
primerContent.bodyCy: Mae AU yn aml yn swnio'n debyg i sain hir “I” Saesneg mewn geiriau fel “sky” neu “tie”.

lists.name: AW
lists.description: AW is another common Welsh vowel pattern. Many learners find it easier than expected because it sounds quite familiar to English speakers.

primerContent.bodyEn: AW often sounds similar to the “ow” in the English word “cow”.
primerContent.bodyCy: Mae AW yn aml yn swnio'n debyg i “ow” yn y gair Saesneg “cow”.

lists.name: U
lists.description: Many learners expect U to behave like English U. In Welsh, it often sounds quite different. With practice, the pattern becomes familiar.

primerContent.bodyEn: In modern Welsh, U often sounds similar to I.
primerContent.bodyCy: Yn y Gymraeg fodern, mae U yn aml yn swnio'n debyg i I.

lists.name: C
lists.description: Unlike English, Welsh C does not change its sound. This makes Welsh spelling more predictable.

primerContent.bodyEn:
Welsh C always keeps its hard sound.
It sounds like the C in the English word “cat”.
primerContent.bodyCy:
Mae C Gymraeg bob amser yn cadw ei sain galed.
Mae'n swnio fel y C yn y gair Saesneg “cat”.

lists.name: G
lists.description: Welsh G behaves consistently. It does not switch between hard and soft sounds as it sometimes does in English.

primerContent.bodyEn: Welsh G always keeps its hard sound.
primerContent.bodyCy: Mae G Gymraeg bob amser yn cadw ei sain galed.

lists.name: TH vs DD
lists.description: TH and DD are often confused by English speakers. Learning to hear the difference makes many Welsh words easier to recognise and spell.

primerContent.bodyEn:
DD sounds like the TH in the English word “then”.
TH sounds like the TH in the English word “think”.
primerContent.bodyCy:
Mae DD yn swnio fel TH yn y gair Saesneg “then”.
Mae TH yn swnio fel TH yn y gair Saesneg “think”.

lists.name: Mixed Confidence — Foundations 1
lists.description: Reinforce D / DD, Y, F / FF, and W with a short representative recap.

words.usageNote: FF often sounds like a typical English F.

lists.name: Mixed Confidence — Foundations 2
lists.description: Reinforce CH, LL, RH, and AE / AI with a short representative recap.

lists.name: Mixed Confidence — Foundations 3
lists.description: Reinforce WY, YW, OE, AU, and AW with a short representative recap.

lists.name: Mixed Confidence — Foundations 4
lists.description: Reinforce the final Foundations sound patterns introduced after Mixed Confidence 3.

lists.name: Mixed Confidence — Foundations 5
lists.description: Transfer and confidence: put familiar Foundations spelling patterns together in new and broader words.

lists.name: Mixed Confidence 1
lists.description: These words combine several patterns from earlier lessons. The goal is confidence, not perfection.

primerContent.bodyEn:
This review combines several patterns you have already practised.
There are no new rules here.
Simply notice how familiar patterns start working together.
primerContent.bodyCy:
Mae'r adolygiad hwn yn cyfuno sawl patrwm rydych eisoes wedi'u hymarfer.
Nid oes rheolau newydd yma.
Sylwch sut mae patrymau cyfarwydd yn dechrau gweithio gyda'i gilydd.

lists.name: Mixed Confidence 2
lists.description: Many of these words contain patterns you have already learned. Try noticing how those patterns become easier to recognise.

primerContent.bodyEn: This review introduces longer words and a wider mixture of familiar spelling patterns.
primerContent.bodyCy: Mae'r adolygiad hwn yn cyflwyno geiriau hirach a chymysgedd ehangach o batrymau sillafu cyfarwydd.

lists.name: Mixed Confidence 3
lists.description: By now you have seen many of the most important Welsh spelling patterns. The goal is to recognise them naturally when they appear together.

primerContent.bodyEn: This review combines many of the patterns introduced throughout Foundations.
primerContent.bodyCy: Mae'r adolygiad hwn yn cyfuno llawer o'r patrymau a gyflwynwyd drwy gydol Foundations.

lists.name: Mixed Confidence 4
lists.description: These words are intended to feel slightly more natural and less controlled than the earlier lessons. Notice how many familiar patterns now appear automatically without needing to think about them.

primerContent.bodyEn:
These words introduce a broader range of familiar Welsh spelling patterns.
There are no new rules to learn.
Instead, use them as an opportunity to recognise patterns you have already practised.
primerContent.bodyCy:
Mae'r geiriau hyn yn cyflwyno ystod ehangach o batrymau sillafu Cymraeg cyfarwydd.
Nid oes rheolau newydd i'w dysgu.
Yn hytrach, defnyddiwch nhw fel cyfle i adnabod patrymau rydych eisoes wedi'u hymarfer.
